blocks.text: Re: Organization Design for Stark-Wilson
Dear Ms. Gonzalez:

This letter sets down the terms on which Calderwood Partners will carry out the organization design work we discussed with you and your team over the past several weeks. When you have signed and returned it, it becomes the agreement between your company and this firm, and it takes the place of the proposal and the conversations that came before it. I have kept it short and put the terms that matter most to you near the front, so you can read them first and the boilerplate after.
The Work

The engagement is about how your company is organized to operate: the shape of the reporting structure, where decisions are made and who owns them, the accountabilities carried at each level, and whether the design you run today fits the direction the business is taking. We will deliver three things. The first is a diagnostic of the organization as it runs now, built from your own charts, your management reporting, and interviews with the people who run the operation day to day. The second is a target design, meaning a structure, a set of decision rights, and the governance to hold them, drawn for where the company is going rather than for where it has been. The third is a plan to move from the first to the second, sequenced so the changes that return the most come first and the disruption is spread on purpose rather than by accident.

A few matters sit deliberately outside this work, and we can price them separately if you want them. We will recommend changes to roles and reporting lines; we will not make the personnel decisions that follow, which stay yours to take. We are not engaged to select or install systems, to advise on tax or legal structure, or to negotiate with anyone on your behalf. Where our analysis runs into a question we have not been asked to answer, we will say so and stop there, instead of answering it by guessing and calling the guess a finding.
Who Does the Work

Julie Parks, our Senior Engagement Manager, runs the engagement day to day and is your first point of contact for anything on it. Deborah Gordon carries much of the analysis and the fieldwork, and she will be in the interviews and behind the drafts. I read the diagnostic and the recommendation before either is put in front of you, as I do with every piece of work that leaves this firm. If the people on your engagement have to change, you will hear it from us before it happens, not after the fact.
Fee, Payment, and Start

Our fee for the engagement is a fixed fee of {{fact:f:E-2011-002.fee}}, which covers the work described above and the team named to do it. We bill monthly, in arrears, for the portion of the work completed in the preceding month, and each invoice is due within thirty days of its date. Reasonable out-of-pocket costs, where there are any, are billed at cost and itemized on the invoice. Work that falls outside the scope set out above is never begun on assumption; if you ask for something this letter does not cover, we will price it and wait for your written go-ahead before we start. The engagement commences on {{fact:f:E-2011-002.start}}, subject to your signature on this letter and to our receiving the current organization charts, your most recent management reporting, and access to the leaders we will need to interview.
Term and Notice

This agreement takes effect when you sign below and runs until we have delivered the implementation plan and rendered our final invoice. Either of us may end it earlier on thirty days' written notice to the other. If it ends before the work is finished, you will owe us for the work performed and the costs incurred to the effective date, and we will hand over what we have in the state it then stands, with a written note of what is left undone. Work delivered part finished is exactly that, and it should not be read as a completed recommendation.
Confidential Information

We will treat the information you give us as confidential and use it only for this engagement. Inside the firm, access is held to the team named above and to the few others whose work requires it. We will not pass your information to anyone outside the firm without your written consent, unless a court or a regulator with authority over it compels us, and in that event we will tell you first if we are free to. Our work product is prepared for your management's use on this matter; it is not to be relied on by anyone else, or on any other matter, without our written agreement. These obligations outlast the engagement itself.
Limits on Our Liability

Our total liability to you arising out of this engagement, whether the claim is framed in contract, in tort, or otherwise, will not exceed the fees you have actually paid us under it, save in the case of our own fraud or willful misconduct. Neither party will be liable to the other for indirect or consequential loss, lost profits among it, whether or not either of us foresaw the possibility. Our conclusions rest on the accuracy and completeness of what you furnish; where that information proves materially wrong, the consequences of the error are not ours to carry, though we will tell you promptly whenever we have reason to doubt what we have been handed.
Governing Law

This agreement is governed by the law of the state in which our principal office sits, without regard to its conflict-of-laws rules. Should a dispute arise, we will each put it first to a senior representative of the two sides and try to settle it in conversation before either of us begins formal proceedings. This letter, once signed, is the whole of the agreement between us on its subject, and it may be amended only in a writing that both of us sign.

If these terms match your understanding, please sign both copies of this letter where indicated, keep one for your records, and return the other to me. If any of them does not, telephone me and we will settle it before you sign rather than after. We are glad to be taking on this work for Stark-Wilson, and we will be ready to begin the interviews as soon as the signed letter is back with us.
Sincerely,

To: Organization Design engagement team
From: Julie Parks
Re: Stark-Wilson kickoff

Where we stand: the letter is signed, our start date is {{fact:f:E-2011-002.start}}, and {{fact:f:E-2011-002.client}} has named Julie Gonzalez, their Director of Operations, as our sponsor and the person who clears our access on site. The engagement is an organization design. We diagnose how the company is run today, put forward a target structure with its decision rights and governance, and set out a plan to move from one to the other. This memo lays out the tracks of work and who owns each of them in the opening weeks. Read the ownership as a starting point, not as walls; the first weeks are small enough that most of us will touch most of it. None of this is a finding yet. The job right now is to get the data in, get the interviews booked, and set the baseline on a footing we can defend later.

We work from their numbers, not from a design carried in from another client. Anything we later put in front of the leadership has to trace back to a chart or a report they already keep, so keep the early notes clean and sourced from the day they come in. That discipline is slower at the start, and it is what makes the recommendation hold when someone pushes hard on it later.

On the client side, Julie Gonzalez is our single point of contact for data and access. Route requests through her rather than going straight to the functions. It keeps the asks coordinated, and it keeps her able to answer for what we have and have not been given. She has also asked that we not characterize the structure to the business-unit heads before their own interviews, so the sessions stay diagnostic instead of turning into a defense of boxes. Hold to that.
Tracks

items: Baseline. Collect the current organization charts and the last two years of management reporting, then build a headcount and cost view that ties back to figures the client already recognizes. I will route the data request through Julie Gonzalez so it carries her authority when it lands with the functions. Every number we later show has to trace to a line in their own reporting, so log the source as you go.

Interviews. Build the interview list from the leadership team and book the first round. Deborah Gordon owns the schedule and will sit in on every session, so the notes stay consistent no matter who is asking the questions. Keep the questions the same across interviewers; we want to compare answers, not styles.

Decision rights. Lay the current decision rights against the reporting structure and mark every place the two disagree. Deborah Gordon takes the first draft of the map; I will review it before any of it goes near the client. The gaps are the point, so note who actually makes each call, not who the chart says should.

Governance. Catalog the standing meetings and committees and what each one is meant to decide. This feeds the target design and can run a step behind the other three without holding anything up, but do not let it drop; the committees are where the drift usually hides.

blocks.text: First two weeks

items: Data request to Julie Gonzalez by Friday, with the exact file list attached so nothing comes back half filled.
Interview list drafted and circulated to the team for comment before a single invitation goes out.
Leadership kickoff call scheduled for the first full week we are on site.
Team check-in on the calendar, thirty minutes, twice a week, so no question waits a week for an answer.

blocks.text: Flag anything unclear before Friday so the data request goes out clean the first time. Once the charts and the reporting are in, we meet to sort the first cut of the baseline and to fix the interview order from what the numbers show.
Julie Gonzalez, with Deborah Gordon copied:

A short update and one request. We have the organization charts and the first cut of the management reporting for Stark-Wilson, and Deborah Gordon and I have started building the headcount and cost picture off them. The reconciliation to your reported totals is already close, so the baseline is on solid ground before the interviews even begin. Deborah Gordon is setting the interview schedule with your leadership team now, and the first sessions should land the week after next.

What we still need is the reporting broken out by cost center rather than by division. Either form works to get going, but the cost-center view is the one that lets us tie spend to the structure, which is the whole point of the baseline. If your finance group can pull that cut for {{fact:f:E-2011-002.client}}, it saves us a reconciliation step later and keeps the numbers you eventually see tied to accounts you already keep. A rough pull is fine to start; we do not need it polished, only complete.

Nothing else is needed from you today. I will send the interview list tomorrow with proposed times, so your assistants can hold the slots before the calendars fill. If there is anyone you would rather we not approach until later, mark them and we will hold off.
Julie Parks
Julie Gonzalez, with Deborah Gordon copied:

Thank you for turning the cost-center reporting around so quickly. It came in last night, and the totals tie to the divisional cut we already had, so the baseline can go ahead on the footing we wanted and we do not lose the week I was worried about.

Two things before the working session next week. Deborah Gordon has the interview list ready and will send times to your office today; if any of the names or the slots look wrong to you, say so before the first session and we will move them without any fuss. We are also putting together a short briefing for that session, so you can see where the baseline stands, what the early numbers suggest, and what the interviews are meant to settle. It will be tight, a handful of pages rather than a deck for its own sake, and it is meant to start a conversation, not close one.

For {{fact:f:E-2011-002.client}} we are about where we wanted to be at this point: charts in, reporting in, interviews set. I will confirm the session time as soon as Stark-Wilson's calendar is back to me, and I will copy Deborah Gordon so she can build the interview week around it.
Julie Parks
Organization Design for Stark-Wilson

items: Client sponsor: Julie Gonzalez, Director of Operations, {{fact:f:E-2011-002.client}}
Calderwood engagement team: Julie Parks and Deborah Gordon
Start of work: {{fact:f:E-2011-002.start}}
Progress briefing for the client, early in the engagement

blocks.text: The Mandate

items: Diagnose how the company is organized to run today, layer by layer and decision by decision
Design a target structure with clear decision rights and the governance to hold them
Sequence the change into a plan the business can act on without stalling the operation
Work from the company's own charts and reporting, not a template carried in from another client

blocks.text: How We Work

items: Build the baseline from your own charts and reporting, so every figure traces to a source you already keep
Interview the leadership on how decisions are actually made, not only how the chart says they should be
Route data and access through a single sponsor, Julie Gonzalez, to keep the requests coordinated
Check in with your office each week, so nothing waits for the next formal milestone to surface

blocks.text: Where the Work Stands

items: Organization charts and two years of management reporting received and logged
Cost and headcount baseline under construction, tied line by line to the company's own accounts
Cost-center reporting now in hand, so spend can be tied to the structure rather than the balance sheet
Interview schedule set with the leadership team; first sessions beginning this month

blocks.text: What the Diagnostic Will Settle

items: Whether the reporting layers match the work the headcount actually carries
Where decision rights on the chart differ from how decisions are made in practice
Which parts of the current structure fit where the business is going, and which are holding it back
What the current design costs, once the soft internal allocations are cleaned up

blocks.text: Next Steps

items: Complete the leadership interviews over the next two weeks
Finish the cost baseline and reconcile it fully to the management accounts
Bring the first current-state findings back for review before the diagnostic is drafted
Deborah Gordon coordinates the scheduling; Julie Parks is the point of contact for anything on the engagement

blocks.text: Working Session: Organization Design for Stark-Wilson
Minutes recorded by Deborah Gordon. Subject: {{fact:f:E-2011-002.client}} organization design, working session at the midpoint of the engagement.
Attendees

items: Julie Parks, Senior Engagement Manager
Deborah Gordon, Associate
Julie Gonzalez, Director of Operations, Stark-Wilson

blocks.text: Current-State Picture

The through-line of the session was blunt: the structure carries more layers than the work needs, and the leadership already feels it even where they cannot yet put a name to it. We walked the current-state picture built from the charts, the reconciled baseline, and the interviews that are now nearly done. Julie Parks framed the hour around a single question for the client, which was whether the current state reads as we have described it before we carry any of it into the design. It did, with two corrections that we logged on the spot. Neither correction moved the shape of the finding; both sharpened it. It is worth saying plainly that nothing here is settled by the numbers alone. The baseline tells us where the layers and the cost sit; the interviews tell us why they sit there, and the why is what the design will have to answer.
Baseline and Decision Rights

On the cost baseline, the reconciliation to the management accounts now ties at the function level, and the layer analysis holds wherever the data is clean. Two functions still run soft on the internal allocation, and we are holding those numbers back a week rather than publish figures we already expect to move. Julie Gonzalez confirmed the pattern from the operations side without prompting: on several routine approvals the decision sits two levels above where the chart places it, and she offered two examples we had not captured. Both went straight into the decision-rights map. The sharper point she made was that the drift is not laziness. It grew up because the formal owner sat too far from the work to decide quickly, so people routed around the delay until the workaround became the real process. That is a design problem, not a discipline problem, and it changes how we frame the fix: move the decision to where the work is, rather than scold the people who moved it there first.
Boundaries and What Comes Next

None of this is a recommendation yet. It is the current state, described and sourced, and the design work starts from exactly here and not a step before. We agreed again to keep the findings inside the room until the last interviews are finished, so the remaining sessions stay diagnostic and do not curdle into a negotiation over who loses a box. Julie Parks will close out the interviews on the firm's side; Julie Gonzalez will keep the leadership expecting questions rather than conclusions when we sit down with them. The next milestone is the diagnostic draft, and the target-design options build out from that.
Action Items

rows: Close the internal allocation for the two soft functions and refresh the layer view
Complete the remaining leadership interviews and log the outstanding decision-rights gaps
Send the two additional approval examples with the names attached to them
Draft the current-state diagnostic once the baseline and the interviews are final
